does: This paper addresses the prediction-execution mismatch in asynchronous Vision-Language-Action policies by proposing DEFLECT, an offline post-training framework that converts latency-induced misalignment into counterfactual preference supervision. The core method uses a frozen reference VLA to generate preferred action chunks from future execution-time observations and rejected chunks from stale prediction-time observations, then trains the policy to score both chunks under deployment-time inputs and favor the execution-time-aligned actions. This offline approach avoids runtime repair overhead while directly teaching the policy to handle the temporal mismatch that naturally arises when inference latency causes actions to be computed from outdated scene information but executed after the scene has evolved.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature of asynchronous inference in robotic control that aims to hide large-model latency behind ongoing motion, extending beyond synchronous action-chunk methods. It connects to preference-learning and preference-alignment approaches in imitation learning and reinforcement learning that use human feedback or synthetic preferences to refine policies, while differing by using temporal counterfactuals based on actual execution dynamics rather than external evaluations. The paper responds to limitations in existing behavior-cloning and runtime repair techniques that do not explicitly account for the systematic temporal shift between prediction time and execution time in asynchronous systems. It also relates to broader work on robustness in learning-based control and distribution shift in vision-language models.
enables: This work enables more practical deployment of large-scale VLA models in robotics by improving robustness to inference latency without requiring synchronous execution or runtime overhead. It opens directions for designing VLA training objectives that are inherently robust to deployment conditions rather than requiring post-hoc adaptation. The counterfactual preference framework could be extended to other temporal misalignment problems in embodied AI, such as multi-step lookahead planning or handling variable latency across different hardware. Future research could explore online versions of DEFLECT that adapt to changing latency, or integrate the approach with active learning to prioritize retraining on uncertain, delay-sensitive scenarios. The method also suggests potential improvements to VLA pretraining to produce inherently delay-robust policies.
